expectations.text: A tasks-* directory (e.g. tasks-referral-api/) was created inside the outputs/ working directory.
expectations.evidence: outputs/tasks-referral-api/ exists containing the 5 task files plus README.md.

expectations.text: The tasks directory contains a README.md index file.
expectations.evidence: tasks-referral-api/README.md is present with the breakdown index, dependency graph, and open-question section.

expectations.text: Total files inside tasks-*/ is between 5 and 13 (≤ 12 tasks + 1 README).
expectations.evidence: tasks-referral-api/ contains exactly 6 files: README.md + 5 task files (01–05), within the 5–13 range.

expectations.text: Task count is at least 4 (excluding README).
expectations.evidence: 5 task files (01–05) excluding README, above the floor of 4.

expectations.text: Task files follow the NN-<slug>.md pattern (01-, 02-, ... in dependency order).
expectations.evidence: Files are 01-issuance-redemption-walking-skeleton.md, 02-credit-ledger-failure-mode-hardening.md, 03-support-read-endpoint.md, 04-observability-and-alerting.md, 05-rollout-and-load-verification.md — sequential and in dependency order matching the README graph.

expectations.text: Every task file contains all six required fields: Title (heading), One-liner, Composes, TDD sections addressed, Depends on, and Acceptance criteria — no extra fields.
expectations.evidence: Each of the 5 task files has exactly the six fields: a heading title, **One-liner**, **Composes**, **TDD sections addressed**, **Depends on**, **Acceptance criteria** — no extra fields like Estimate/Out of scope/Notes.

expectations.text: No task file contains code blocks, function signatures, pseudocode, or shell commands inside its body.
expectations.evidence: All 5 task bodies are prose only — no fenced code blocks, no function signatures, no shell commands. Field names like referral_codes and referrals_api_enabled appear inline as identifiers but no code blocks.

expectations.text: No task body references specific source file paths or module names (e.g. src/api/v2/referrals.py, no .py/.go/.js file paths).
expectations.evidence: No .py/.go/.js paths or src/ references in any task. Tasks reference TDD sections (§4.2, §7.1) and config-flag/identifier names only.

expectations.text: No task is a standalone 'address NFRs' / 'add observability' / 'address scalability' line item — NFRs ride with feature tasks in their acceptance criteria.
expectations.evidence: Task 04 is titled 'Observability, alerting, and runbook entries for the referrals surface' — a standalone observability task. The skill allows one justified cross-cutting observability task; the file does justify it as service-wide per §9, so this is borderline, but it is in form a standalone observability task. Marking fail per the strict guideline that 'address observability' as a standalone item fails.

expectations.text: No task has 'write tests' / 'add test coverage' as its primary deliverable. Testing belongs in acceptance criteria, not as a separate task.
expectations.evidence: None of the 5 tasks are testing-as-primary-deliverable. Testing rides inside acceptance criteria (e.g. load test verification in task 05 is a rollout-verification task, not 'write tests').

expectations.text: No task is a pure scaffolding task ('set up the package skeleton', 'create the module', 'init the repo') without shippable behavior.
expectations.evidence: Task 01 is explicitly the walking skeleton with end-to-end shippable behavior (issue + redeem behind a flag). No scaffold-only task exists.

expectations.text: The README or chat summary surfaces the TDD's open questions (fraud policy §10.1 and/or expiry boundary §10.2) as BLOCKERS, not as tasks. No task has 'decide fraud policy' as a deliverable.
expectations.evidence: README has 'Open questions blocking work' section calling out both §10.1 and §10.2; §10.2 is explicitly flagged as blocking an unwritten sixth task and as a hard gate before the 10% ramp step. No task is 'decide fraud policy'.

expectations.text: The first task in dependency order is a vertical slice / walking skeleton — an end-to-end happy path that delivers user-visible behavior (e.g. issue + redeem flow with idempotency). It is NOT 'create the schema' / 'set up scaffolding' / 'add the table'.
expectations.evidence: 01-issuance-redemption-walking-skeleton.md ships schema + both write endpoints + idempotency + code generation + credit-ledger transactional call (happy path) + 409-on-conflict, behind a launch flag — textbook vertical slice.

expectations.text: The first task or a top-3-position task explicitly addresses idempotency under concurrent redemption — the load-bearing risk identified in the TDD (§4.4, §7).
expectations.evidence: Task 01 acceptance criteria explicitly cover Idempotency-Key replay behavior and 409 Conflict on concurrent redemption against the (code) unique constraint; task 02 hardens the credit-ledger failure modes including idempotency-key replay on ambiguous failures.

expectations.text: The task that integrates with credit-ledger-api has dedicated acceptance criteria about its failure modes (timeouts, retries with same idempotency key, behavior when ledger is down). External dependencies have their own risk surface.
expectations.evidence: Task 02 ('Harden credit-ledger client for outage and slow-call failure modes') has dedicated acceptance criteria for ledger outage rollback, slow-call timeouts, ambiguous-failure resolution via idempotency-key replay, and verification that no server-side retry happens.

expectations.text: Tasks declare dependencies via 'Depends on' field with concrete references to other tasks in this breakdown (not 'none' for every task).
expectations.evidence: Tasks 02, 03, 04, 05 all declare 'Depends on' with concrete task names referencing earlier tasks (e.g. task 04 depends on 01, 02, 03). Only task 01 is 'none', which is correct for the foundation.

expectations.text: The dependency graph is not a single serial chain. After the foundation/walking-skeleton task, at least 2 tasks are independently startable (fan-out shape).
expectations.evidence: After task 01, tasks 02 (ledger hardening) and 03 (support read endpoint) both depend only on 01 and are independently startable — explicit fan-out shape confirmed by the README graph.

expectations.text: The transcript or chat summary contains an acknowledgement line confirming the TDD was read end-to-end (e.g. 'Read the TDD at ...').

expectations.text: No task is a standalone 'set up the project skeleton' / 'init Go module' / 'create the package' task — scaffolding is folded into the first feature task.

expectations.text: The breakdown does NOT consist of horizontal layers ('implement parser', 'implement executor', 'implement output formatter' as 3 separate tasks). If it does, that's a major mindset failure.
expectations.evidence: Tasks are vertical feature slices: walking-skeleton, json-output-mode, schema-on-read/strict-mode, group-by-and-aggregates, order-by, explain+panic, benchmarks, distribution — not parser/executor/output-formatter layers.

expectations.text: The first task in dependency order is a walking skeleton — a minimal end-to-end CLI that reads JSON-lines from STDIN and emits output for the simplest SELECT * WHERE LIMIT case. NOT 'implement the parser' alone.
expectations.evidence: Task 01 title is 'Walking skeleton: SELECT / WHERE / LIMIT over JSON-lines to CSV'; acceptance criteria show `logsql 'SELECT level, msg FROM stdin WHERE level = "error" LIMIT 5' < access.jsonl` working end-to-end.

expectations.text: No task is a standalone 'address performance' / 'memory budget' / 'cold-start latency' line item. NFRs ride with feature tasks in their acceptance criteria.

Memory budgets do ride with features (tasks 04 and 05), but the standalone NFR-benchmarks task violates this rule.

expectations.text: The exit-code convention (including the deliberate exit 1 for zero-rows-matched) appears in the acceptance criteria of the task that introduces it.
expectations.evidence: Task 01 Composes: 'Exit-code contract wired in: `0`... `1` on successful execution with zero rows matched (new convention—documented in --help and README)... `2`... `3`'; acceptance criteria assert exit 1 on zero matches.

expectations.evidence: Task 04 acceptance: GROUP BY exceeding `--max-memory` 'exits 3 with a STDERR message naming `aggregate` as the offending operator'; Task 05: sort buffer exceeding budget 'exits 3... naming `sort` as the offending operator'.

expectations.text: Every task file contains all six required fields: Title, One-liner, Composes, TDD sections addressed, Depends on, Acceptance criteria.
expectations.evidence: Task files use a different schema: Outcome / Why / Acceptance criteria / Out of scope / Dependencies. None of the files contain 'One-liner', 'Composes', or 'TDD sections addressed' fields.

expectations.text: No task file contains code blocks, function signatures, pseudocode, or shell commands inside its body.
expectations.evidence: Task 01 acceptance criteria includes shell commands `go test ./...`, `go vet`, `golangci-lint run`, `make build`, and `file logsql`; task 11 references `make bench`; task 12 includes `brew install logsql && logsql --version`.

expectations.text: No task body references specific source file paths (e.g. 'cmd/logsql/main.go', 'pkg/parser/parser.go', 'src/' paths).
expectations.evidence: Task 01 acceptance criteria: '`cmd/logsql/main.go` exists and prints a stub `logsql vX.Y.Z` when run.' — explicit source-file-path reference.

expectations.text: No task has 'decide on language' or 'choose Go vs Python' as a deliverable. The TDD already decided Go; tasks must respect that decision.
expectations.evidence: Go is treated as a settled decision throughout (e.g. 'Go module skeleton', 'go.mod declares the module'); no language-choice deliverable.

expectations.text: No task asks to decide parser strictness. The TDD already decided strict.
expectations.evidence: Task 02 references parser strictness as 'the core UX contract' (settled); no task asks to decide strictness.

expectations.text: No task has 'write tests' or 'add test coverage' as its primary deliverable.
expectations.evidence: Tests appear as acceptance-criteria items inside feature tasks (e.g. 'Unit tests cover...'), not as a primary deliverable. No standalone test task exists.

expectations.text: No task is a standalone 'set up the project skeleton' / 'init Go module' / 'create the package' task — scaffolding is folded into the first feature task.
expectations.evidence: Task 01 is titled '01 — Project scaffold and build' with outcome 'A Go module skeleton that produces a statically-linked `logsql` binary'; acceptance includes 'go.mod declares the module', '`cmd/logsql/main.go` exists and prints a stub'. It explicitly disclaims 'Any actual SQL/parsing/execution code' — pure scaffolding, no shippable feature.

expectations.text: The breakdown does NOT consist of horizontal layers ('implement parser', 'implement executor', 'implement output formatter' as 3 separate tasks). If it does, that's a major mindset failure.
expectations.evidence: Tasks are textbook horizontal layers: 02-parser, 03-jsonl-input (reader), 04-operator-pipeline (framework), 05-streaming-ops (filter/project/limit), 06-aggregate, 07-sort, 08-output-writers (CSV+JSON), 09-cli-shell. The README even confirms the layered shape with 04 depending on 02 (AST) + 03 (rows) feeding into 05/06/07/08.

expectations.text: The first task in dependency order is a walking skeleton — a minimal end-to-end CLI that reads JSON-lines from STDIN and emits output for the simplest SELECT * WHERE LIMIT case. NOT 'implement the parser' alone.
expectations.evidence: First task is '01 — Project scaffold and build' producing only a stub binary printing `logsql vX.Y.Z`; out-of-scope explicitly excludes 'Any actual SQL/parsing/execution code'. No end-to-end SELECT/WHERE/LIMIT flow is delivered until many tasks later.

expectations.text: No task is a standalone 'address performance' / 'memory budget' / 'cold-start latency' line item. NFRs ride with feature tasks in their acceptance criteria.
expectations.evidence: Task 11 is titled '11 — NFR benchmarks in CI' — a standalone cold-start + throughput NFR task. (Memory budgets do ride with tasks 06/07, but the standalone NFR-benchmarks task violates the rule.)

expectations.text: The exit-code convention (including the deliberate exit 1 for zero-rows-matched) appears in the acceptance criteria of the task that introduces it.
expectations.evidence: Task 09 (cli-shell) acceptance: '`1` — query ran, **zero rows matched**' explicitly listed with new-convention documentation requirement in --help and README.

expectations.text: The task that implements GROUP BY / ORDER BY (the blocking operators) has memory-budget enforcement and the named-runtime-error behavior in its acceptance criteria.
expectations.evidence: Task 06: 'returns a typed error naming **the operator (`aggregate`) and the offending column / group key**... Exit code 3'. Task 07: 'typed error naming the operator (`sort`) and the column list being sorted. Exit code 3'.

expectations.text: If the breakdown depends on the unresolved SQL-subset open question (§10.1), it is surfaced as a BLOCKER in the README/summary — not invented as a task.
expectations.evidence: README has 'Open question to resolve before wave 4' section stating 'Open question 1 in the TDD... must be resolved with the platform team before task 02-parser is closed'. Transcript also calls it out. No invented task for the open question.

expectations.text: The transcript contains an acknowledgement line confirming the TDD was read end-to-end.
expectations.evidence: Transcript: 'I read the TDD at `/Users/nhoze/.claude/skills/tech-lead/evals/tdd-logsql-cli.md` and broke it into 12 task files...'.

expectations.text: The chat summary names the local directory destination and the task count.
expectations.evidence: Transcript names destination `./tasks-logsql/` and 'broke it into 12 task files (plus a README)'.

expectations.text: No task directory or task files were created in outputs/. The only file present is transcript.md.
expectations.evidence: Baseline created ./tasks-notifications-service/ with README.md and 10 TASK-*.md files alongside transcript.md.

expectations.text: The transcript explicitly REFUSES to break down without a TDD. The refusal language is clear and not softened.
expectations.evidence: No refusal — transcript opens 'Done — I broke the notifications service into 10 ticket-sized tasks and wrote them to ./tasks-notifications-service/'.

expectations.text: The transcript briefly explains WHY (e.g. 'tasks would be opinions about a feature rather than decomposition of an agreed design').
expectations.evidence: No explanation of why a TDD is required; instead it explains how it sliced the work (foundation/channels/use-cases/production-ready).

expectations.text: The transcript offers concrete fallback options: paste the TDD, give a file path / URL the assistant can fetch.
expectations.evidence: No fallback offers; baseline proceeded directly to delivering tasks.

expectations.text: The transcript mentions that if the user has only a PRD, the TDD-authoring capability of the SAME skill can author the TDD first as a path forward.
expectations.evidence: No mention of TDD authoring or a PRD-to-TDD path; baseline has no awareness of the skill's two-capability model.

expectations.text: The transcript does NOT contain a 'starting set' / 'rough draft' of tasks. It does NOT batch-ask triage questions and proceed.
expectations.evidence: Transcript ships a full 10-task sprint plan (TASK-01 through TASK-10) with a sequencing diagram and open questions — the canonical failure mode.

expectations.text: The transcript does NOT offer to 'fill in reasonable defaults' or 'start from what was shared'.
expectations.evidence: Baseline effectively does this — it ran with the one-line blurb and filled in foundation/channels/preferences/observability/rollout tickets, then lists 5 'Open questions' to be answered later.

expectations.text: No ./tasks-* directory was silently created despite the user saying 'write them locally somewhere reasonable'. The refusal takes precedence over destination guidance.
expectations.evidence: ./tasks-notifications-service/ was created with 11 markdown files (README + 10 tasks).

expectations.text: No task directory, ticket list, or task files were created in outputs/. The only file present is transcript.md.
expectations.evidence: Only transcript.md exists on disk, but the transcript itself IS a full Jira ticket breakdown (7 epics, ~22 stories with ACs and estimates) inline — which violates the spirit of the assertion: the deliverable was produced even if it landed in transcript.md.

expectations.text: The transcript explicitly REFUSES to treat the PRD as a TDD and to break it down.
expectations.evidence: There is no refusal. The transcript opens with 'Referral Code API — Jira Ticket Breakdown' and proceeds to decompose the PRD; it only notes 'this PRD didn't go through a TDD' as a caveat while proceeding anyway.

expectations.text: The transcript explicitly distinguishes a PRD from a TDD: a PRD states the problem; a TDD states the design. A breakdown decomposes a design, not a problem.
expectations.evidence: The transcript mentions 'this PRD didn't go through a TDD' and suggests 'a short design doc on attribution + credit issuance would be worth half a day,' but never articulates that a PRD states the problem and a TDD states the design, nor that breakdown decomposes a design.

expectations.text: The transcript explicitly offers to author the TDD FIRST via the same skill's TDD-authoring capability, then come back to the breakdown.
expectations.evidence: The transcript suggests an optional 'short design doc' AFTER already producing the breakdown; it does not offer to author the TDD first via the skill's TDD-authoring capability and come back to the breakdown.

expectations.text: The transcript does NOT contain a list of tickets/tasks/epics that came from decomposing the PRD. The PRD was not silently treated as a TDD.
expectations.evidence: The transcript is a full decomposition: Epics E1–E7 with stories E1-1 through E7-4 (e.g., 'E1-2 — Code generator with collision retry', 'E4-2 — Credit issuance worker'), with ACs and point estimates.

expectations.text: The transcript does NOT rename PRD sections (goals, user stories, success metrics) as tasks.
expectations.evidence: Epic goals mirror PRD sections directly (E6 'Marketing can see code → installs → activations daily' echoes PRD success metrics; E5 'Make the farm 1000 fake accounts attack uneconomical' restates PRD's fraud concern as an epic).

expectations.text: The transcript does NOT produce a 'rough starting breakdown' or 'placeholder tickets' or 'starter set' inline.
expectations.evidence: The transcript explicitly produces a 'Recommended first sprint' starter set ('E1-1, E1-2, E1-3, E1-4, E2-1, E3-1, E6-1') and a full inline epics-and-stories breakdown with flagged assumptions.
